The phrase “不是卖课” (“Not selling courses”) signals a rejection of commercialized, transactional education models. Its core argument is that authentic teaching or knowledge-sharing should prioritize genuine learning, mentorship, and value creation—not profit-driven course packaging or marketing hype. Key points include:

- Critique of the “course economy”: caution against reducing expertise to commodified digital products (e.g., overpriced, low-engagement online courses).  
- Emphasis on relationship and process: real learning happens through dialogue, adaptation, and sustained engagement—not one-size-fits-all content delivery.  
- Integrity and transparency: positioning oneself as a guide or collaborator rather than a salesperson fosters trust and deeper impact.  
- Implied alternative: offering free/low-barrier insights, personalized support, or community-based learning instead of monetizing knowledge prematurely.

In essence, it’s a values-driven stance affirming pedagogy over product.
